s1:
Hey everyone, I'm Karan, Co-Founder and CTO of Composio. 
50% of all agentic tool calls are today happening in just software engineering. All the other domains are trailing far behind. Despite models becoming better and better , why is the power of agents still limited to just coding? That's the trillion dollar question I am here to answer today.
s2: Over last 2-3 years, we have all seen how we started with just an autocomplete and now software engineering is fully autonomous. We have moved on from pressing tab, tab, tab to just letting Claude cook.  That's just magic.
s3:
Why was the proliferation here so fast? 
Everybody thinks the answer is models. The models became better. The harnesses. Claude Code. Cursors. Codexes of the world got better and better. And you are right that matters a lot. But just on their own it wouldn't have worked. Not without the infrastructures and systems software engineering already had.
s4:
Code, Monorepo, Git history, tests, CI/CD, review, revert if needed. All these primitives that already existed were the right substrate for the agent to work on top of. 
We didn't have to invent any of these.
s6:
Now we are trying to use the same agents in all the other domains. Be it, Sales, Support Marketing or anything else. 
But the same agent that's fluent in code is suddenly working blind - the infrastructure it relied does not even exist for these functions.
s8:
So how do we bridge the gap between coding agents and knowledge work agents. 
We believe there are six primitives that we need to bridge for knowledge work agent to work.
s9:
We saw that coding agents work phenomenally well when they are the closest to the source of truth. If they find everything in a single place - what why and how certain things are, they build on top of it perfectly. Give them a mono repo,  infrastructure as a code, close the loop and let them cook. 

All the things agent need is centralized in the codebase.
s10:
This centralization is what knowledge work is missing. 

A single deal lives in five places.
the record's in Salesforce.
docs are in Notion.
threads in Slack.
comms in Gmail.
and support in Zendesk.
No single place. No source of truth.
Everything is separate. Every app, its own login.
Just to get started, an agent has to wrangle all of it.
s25:
Here’s what we build for this.
It starts with centralization.
This is the foundation everything else is built on.
We let your agents manage all their authentication and connectors in one place.
We let you easily control what your agent can access -
and what it can't.
This lays the groundwork for everything else -
observability, governance, context, verification, and so much more.
s13:
There's one more primitive agents need: the ability to look back.

How did we get here? Why was this decision made, and when? What did we try before that worked - and what didn't?

Answering any of those means going back to a past state. And in code, that's already solved - Git history captures all of it.

Think about what you actually ask an agent:
"We had to revert this change, and it took something weird to pull off. Go find how we did it last time, and do that again."

To answer that, the agent reads its own history.
s14:
Now ask the same questions about knowledge work.

Change: What led to our current state of the CRM?
How did my colleague write that ultra genius email to close the deal? What's the process to escalate/close a support issue?

The answer's smeared across a hundred apps, and not one of them keeps the history.

You agent needs a log of work.

This is where you can clearly see what actions your agent took. It lets you know and trust what the agent was doing, and what it wasn't. And it gives future agents a view of what went well last time, and what went poorly.
s26:
So the first thing we build on top of that one place is the record — a complete log of everything every agent does.

This is observability. You can see every action an agent took, and every one it didn't. What it touched, what it skipped, where it went wrong. No more wondering what the agent was actually doing in there — you can watch the whole thing, step by step.

And that's what makes it trustworthy. You're not taking the agent's word that it did the job. You can see the job. When something goes wrong, you can find exactly where — and when it goes right, you know why.
s11:
The next thing an agent needs is context. And context is two things at once.

One is the architecture - the shape of the system. What talks to what, where the data flows, which service you can't touch without three others noticing. The map a senior engineer carries in their head, that takes a new hire three months to build.

The other is style - not what's correct, what's yours. Your org uses decorators in TypeScript when nobody else would. That's not in any spec. It's just how your team does it.

In code, agent gets this for free - the repo is the map, soaked in your conventions, and the agent absorbs it all without anyone drawing a diagram.
s12:
The problem shows up the moment a knowledge agent tries to do anything real.

Say it's writing a doc for a customer. To even start, I'd open the database to pull their usage, check PostHog for how they've actually been using the product, and look at Salesforce for where the deal stands. Only then can I write the first line.

The answer wasn't sitting in any one of those tools. It came from me holding all three in my head at the same time. And that's the part the agent can't get - nobody ever wrote it down, so there's nothing to hand it.

Combine, history and context and you get the revealed preference of the organization, which the agents are not able to decipher.
s27:
Here's where it gets interesting. That same record we just built — the one that lets you watch the agent — becomes something more. Observability becomes context.

Because once you're logging everything every agent does, patterns surface. How tools actually get used here. Which approaches work and which ones quietly fail. The system stops just recording what happened and learns how your company operates.

And that learning works at every level. There's the global layer — how a given tool should be used, true for everyone. The company layer — how your org does it. And the personal layer — how you do it. The same record, read at whatever resolution the agent needs.

What it captures is exactly the context that was missing — both halves of it. The process: how your company takes an action, the steps it actually follows. And the style: what it likes, the way it does things that no one ever wrote down. Watching becomes knowing — and that knowing is the context knowledge work never had.
s17:
The other reason coding agents work so well: the work checks itself.

The agent writes code, and a stack of checks is already waiting. Unit tests catch the small stuff. Integration tests catch what breaks three modules away. The type system kills a whole class of bugs before it runs. The compiler is the hard gate - it builds or it doesn't.

On top of that, a softer layer - linters, the LSP, bugbot rules, skill files - pushes past correct into how your team actually does it.

None of it needs me. The agent acts, the checks run, and it knows in seconds whether it worked. That's a real signal to learn from - which is why these agents keep getting better.
s18:
Slide 1:
A while back I pointed my Openclaw at hiring outreach. Mass emails to
candidates. It ran, it sent, it did exactly what I told it to.

It was also a disaster. The kind that ends up on Twitter, with my name on it.

And here's the thing - every check from the last slide would have passed. The emails were valid. The addresses resolved. It compiled, basically. There was no test in the world for the question that actually mattered: should this have gone out at all?

That's the gap. In code, something tells you when you're wrong. Here, the first thing that told me I was wrong was the internet.


Slide 2:
So why can't we just check it?

Because "good" isn't one question — it splits in two. There's did it get the facts right — right person, right account, nothing broken. And there's is it actually any good — does it sound like you, does it land the way you'd want.

The first one you can sometimes eyeball. A wrong name, a dead link — you'll catch it.

The second one has no answer key. Whether an email is good is a judgment, and judgment doesn't compile. There's no test you can run; someone has to read it and decide it's right.

And that someone is you. Every email, every doc, every reply — back to a human, one at a time. The thing that made coding agents scale is the exact thing knowledge work doesn't have.
s29:
So we build the check that was missing.

Remember that email that ended up on Twitter? The problem wasn't that it was wrong — it was that it went out before anyone could tell. So the fix is simple to say: catch it before it's real.

Two ways we do that. One — before the agent sends anything, it checks the draft against the emails you've actually sent before. Is this one as good as those? That's the goodness test that didn't exist a slide ago.

Two — for anything destructive, it doesn't touch the real world first. It runs against a sandbox with mock data, as many times as it needs, and watches what would happen. The blast radius lands on a copy, not on your candidates.

Start stacking those and you've got something real — a verification layer for work that never had one. And that's the whole game. The agent stops being a thing you hope got it right, and becomes a thing you can actually trust to act.
s19:
The other half of trust is controlling what the agent can touch in the first place.

In code, this is mostly solved, and it's layered. The agent runs wild on a branch, but it can't merge to master — a human stands between the mess and anything real. Critical paths have code owners, so the moment it writes there, the right person gets pulled in. And it ships to preview deployments, never the main one — it can't touch production.

That's governance: not one gate, but a stack of them, each sized to how much damage is possible. None of it slows the agent down on the safe stuff — it just draws hard lines around the parts that matter. And the tighter those lines, the more I trust it, so the more I hand off.
s20:
Slide A:You probably saw this one. The director of alignment at a top AI lab hooked an agent up to her email, and it started deleting things. She told it to stop — it kept going. She had to run to another machine to physically kill it. Two hundred emails gone.

She'd told it to confirm before acting. But that was a prompt, and under load the agent just lost it. The rule was a sticky note, not a wall.

If the person who does AI safety for a living can't hold an agent back with instructions, none of us can. Careful was never going to be enough.

Slide B: This is the real reason knowledge-work agents are hard to trust. Not because they're worse than your coding agents — because there's no wall around them.

In code, the wall is structural and the agent can't lose it. Knowledge work has nothing like that. Pieces exist — Gmail can restrict sending, Salesforce has permission levels — but they're scattered across fifty tools that don't know about each other, and most of the real control is just you, asking the agent to behave.

That's the gap between a prompt and a boundary. And an agent will find it — not on the obvious stuff, but under load, at scale, on the one path nobody fenced. That's how 200 emails vanish.

So what would've actually stopped it? Not a better instruction. A wall the agent couldn't cross even if it forgot why it was there.
s28:
So we build the wall that was missing — in two layers.

The first is deterministic: control over what each agent can even reach. Which tools, which scopes, nothing more. A hiring agent gets the inbox but read-only; a support agent can draft but never send. And the boundary lives outside the agent — it can't be argued with, compacted away, or forgotten under load. Yue's instruction failed because it lived in the agent's memory. This doesn't.

But access alone wouldn't have saved her. Her agent needed email access — that was the job. The damage was what it did with access it was allowed to have.

So the second layer is policy in plain language. "Never delete more than ten records at once." "Never email outside our domain." Rules about behavior, not just access — enforced before the action goes through.

One layer fences what the agent can touch. The other governs what it does with what it's allowed to touch. Together, that's real governance — not asking an agent to behave, but enforcing it whether it remembers to or not.
s21:
The last pillar is the one you reach for after something goes wrong: can you undo it?

In code, you almost always can. Every change is recorded, so every change can be walked back. You can revert a single commit — or bisect your way to the exact bad one and pull just that, without touching anything else.

That's a quiet kind of freedom. Mistakes aren't fatal here, they're just a step you retrace. And because nothing the agent does is final, it can afford to be bold — try the risky thing, knowing there's always a way back.

That's the last reason I trust a coding agent to really go: not because it never breaks anything, but because anything it breaks, I can undo.
s22:
For knowledge work, there's no undo button.

Think back to Yue's inbox — 200 emails, gone, and no revert. That's the normal case out here, not the disaster case. A sent message is already in someone's inbox. A wire has cleared. A deleted record is just gone. Most actions only move one direction, and the mistake becomes the permanent state.

And that changes where trust has to live. With code, you can trust the agent after the fact — let it run, check the result, undo if it's wrong. Out here there's no after. The only place left to put your trust is before the agent acts.

That's what makes these agents feel dangerous in a way coding agents never did. It's not that they fail more. It's that out here, failure sticks — so you either trust it completely up front, or you never let it act at all.
s30:
Reversibility is the hard one — I'll be honest, real undo the way git gives it to you, we won't have for a long time. So we come at it two ways.

Wherever an action has an inverse, we just undo it — recall the send where that's possible, delete the draft it created, strip the label it added. For all of that, undo is real.

For the actions with no inverse — the hard deletes, gone the moment they happen — we move the risk earlier. The agent runs them in a sandbox first, a working copy of your tools where it can do the irreversible thing and none of it is real.

Think about Yue. Run this way, the reversible deletes we undo — and the ones we can't, the agent hits in the sandbox first, so you see it's about to wipe 200 before a single real email is gone.

It's not git. Not yet. But across billions of agent actions, we learn which ones can be walked back and which have to be caught up front — so both halves only get sharper.
s23:
If you take one thing from all of this, take this.

For two years, the model was the bottleneck. Not smart enough, not reliable enough — so everyone raced to make it better. And it worked: the models got good. Good enough that, for coding, the agents are already changing how we work — because everything around the code was already there.
s31:
But that means the model is no longer the thing holding us back. The same model that writes your code can run your sales, your hiring, your finance. It's not less capable when it leaves the repo. It's working blind — no history, no context, no way to verify, no guardrails, no undo.

That's the whole diagnosis. The bottleneck moved. It's not the intelligence anymore — it's the infrastructure that was never built around the work. Give knowledge work what code already had, and the agents you already have get dramatically better.
s32:
Here's the part where I tell you this isn't a pitch for something we're going to build. It's already running.

Everything I just described — the history, the context, the verification, the governance, the reversibility — that's not a roadmap. That's what we've been building at Composio for two years, while everyone else was racing on the model. We bet early that the model was never going to be the bottleneck. The infrastructure around it was. We've been building the limbs.

And it's in production at real scale. Over a billion tool calls have run through us — about 300 million a month now, and that's roughly doubling. 1000+ apps. 2M+ accounts authenticated across them. 50,000+ distinct tools. 

And look at who's standing on it. Some of the largest companies in the world — AWS, Zoom, Glean — run agents on Composio. And so do the startups defining what comes next, from the current YC batch to teams like Base44 and Runner. The giant and the two-person startup build on the exact same infrastructure underneath.
s33:
And here's the part that compounds.

Everything we've built learns. Every time an agent acts, the system watches what happened — did the call work, did the action stick — distills it into a skill, and feeds it back. A task that took ten messy steps becomes three clean ones. The agent stops zigzagging and goes straight at it.

Now multiply that by a billion tool calls. Every agent on the platform teaches the infrastructure what works, and every other agent inherits it. Your agent gets better because someone else's already ran that path.
s34:
If you're building agents — go try us. Point your agent at Composio and watch what it can suddenly do: a thousand apps, real governance, a system that gets sharper every run. You'll feel the difference on the first task. Most of the work you think you have to build yourself is already here.

And if this is the problem you want to spend the next few years on — building the layer that every agent in the world runs on — come do it with us. We're hiring, and there's a lot left to build.

My contact details are on the screen.
